message:
fix(packs+context): close 4 critical issues uncovered by red-team review

Fourth fresh-eyes pass uncovered ONE production-breaking false positive
and THREE real bypass vectors. All four shipped on main as part of the
nqhi.4 work yesterday and need urgent fixing.

== ISSUE 1 (CRITICAL false positive): every `> /dev/null` blocked ==

The `redirect-truncate-root-home` rule had `dev` in its sensitive-path
set, so the most common shell idiom in existence — discarding output
to /dev/null — was being blocked. Affected: `cmd > /dev/null`,
`cmd 2>&1 > /dev/null`, `make > /dev/null 2>&1`, `find . > /dev/null`,
literally every script that suppresses output. This shipped in
dc9eed4 yesterday and has been live for hours.

Fix: add `(?!/dev/(?:null|zero|full)\b)` negative lookahead in the
destructive regex. The carve-out is narrow: actual device-target
redirects (`> /dev/sda`, `> /dev/nvme0n1`) still block correctly —
only the standard discard-sinks (null, zero, full) are exempted.

== ISSUE 2 (CRITICAL bypass): glued operator+target ==

`echo data>/etc/passwd` (no space between `>` and path) bypassed the
rule entirely. The dcg tokenizer doesn't break on `>`, so the whole
`data>/etc/passwd` arrives at sanitize as one token; args-data masking
then erased the entire token to spaces, hiding the destructive target
from the regex. Same shape: `echo "data">/etc/passwd`,
`echo data>~/.ssh/id_rsa`, `printf data>/etc/passwd`,
`echo x 2>/etc/passwd`, `echo x &>/etc/passwd`.

Fix: new `glued_redirect_split_position` helper in `src/context.rs`
detects tokens containing `>` followed by a path-start character
(`/`, `~`, `$`, `"`, `'`). When found, sanitize masks ONLY the prefix
before the operator, leaving the operator+target visible. The
heuristic is narrow — `>` followed by a non-path char (`>3`, `>a`,
`> `) still gets fully masked, so plain-data arrows like
`echo "user>admin"` and `echo "5 > 3"` stay safe from rule-fires.

== ISSUE 3 + 4 (HIGH bypasses): ANSI-C and locale quoting ==

`mv $'/etc' /tmp/x` and `> $'/etc/passwd'` bypassed via Bash's
ANSI-C-quoted (`$'...'`) and locale-translated (`$"..."`) string
forms. The optional-quote prefix `['"\\]?` in both regexes only
accepted single-byte quotes — the two-byte `$'` / `$"` introducers
slipped through. Particularly impactful for mv and redirect because
neither has a -general tier to fall back on.

Fix: extend the optional-quote group to `(?:['"\\]|\$['"])?` in both
`mv-sensitive-source-root-home` and `redirect-truncate-root-home`.
This accepts the historical single-char quotes AND the new two-byte
`$'`/`$"` introducers.

== Tests added ==

Six new unit tests in filesystem.rs cover the regression surface:
- `redirect_to_dev_null_zero_full_is_allowed_universally` — guards
  against re-introducing the /dev/null FP. Tests 10 common forms.
- `redirect_to_dev_devices_still_blocks` — proves the carve-out is
  narrow (sda/sdb/nvme still block).
- `redirect_glued_operator_blocks_destructive` — 11 glued-operator
  bypass attempts, all must block.
- `redirect_glued_operator_to_non_sensitive_is_allowed` — proves the
  split heuristic doesn't false-positive on legitimate non-sensitive
  glued redirects.
- `redirect_ansi_c_and_locale_quoted_paths_block` — ANSI-C / locale
  quoting bypass guards.
- `mv_ansi_c_and_locale_quoted_sources_block` — same for mv.
- `echo_quoted_data_args_with_arrow_no_path_dont_falsely_match` —
  guards against the split heuristic over-firing on plain-data arrows
  in echo args (`echo "user>admin"` etc.).

Two new e2e scenarios (27 assertions) plus 10 added to existing
`scenario_redirect_temp_safe` for the /dev/null carve-out coverage.

== Verification ==

All 4 issues now behave correctly:
- `cmd > /dev/null` → ALLOW (was DENY)
- `echo data>/etc/passwd` → DENY (was ALLOW)
- `mv $'/etc' /tmp/x` → DENY (was ALLOW)
- `> $'/etc/passwd'` → DENY (was ALLOW)

2727 lib tests pass; 512 e2e assertions pass (+27 new); cargo fmt +
clippy clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -887,6 +887,57 @@ scenario_redirect_temp_safe() {
     assert_allowed 'echo x 1>&2'
     assert_allowed 'echo x 2>&1'
     assert_allowed 'command 2>&1 | tee log.txt'
+    # CRITICAL regression guard: `> /dev/null` (and zero/full) is the
+    # most common shell idiom in existence. The
+    # `(?!/dev/(?:null|zero|full)\b)` carve-out in the destructive
+    # regex must keep these allowed even though /dev is in the
+    # sensitive set.
+    assert_allowed 'cmd > /dev/null'
+    assert_allowed 'cmd >/dev/null'
+    assert_allowed 'cat /etc/passwd > /dev/null'
+    assert_allowed 'curl https://example.com > /dev/null'
+    assert_allowed 'find . > /dev/null 2>&1'
+    assert_allowed 'cmd > /dev/null 2>&1'
+    assert_allowed 'cmd 2> /dev/null'
+    assert_allowed 'cmd &> /dev/null'
+    assert_allowed 'make > /dev/zero'
+    assert_allowed 'echo test > /dev/full'
+}
+
+scenario_redirect_bypass_attempts() {
+    # Bypass-attempt regression guards. These previously slipped past
+    # the destructive regex due to (a) the dcg tokenizer not splitting
+    # glued operator+target words, and (b) the optional-quote prefix
+    # not accepting Bash ANSI-C (`$'...'`) or locale (`$"..."`) forms.
+    #
+    # Glued operator+target (no space between `>` and path).
+    assert_blocked 'echo data>/etc/passwd'              'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'printf data>/etc/passwd'            'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'echo data>~/.ssh/id_rsa'            'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'echo data>$HOME/.aws/credentials'   'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'echo "data">/etc/passwd'            'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked "echo data>'/etc/passwd'"            'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Numbered FD glued (`2>/path`).
+    assert_blocked 'echo x 1>/etc/passwd'               'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'echo x 2>/etc/passwd'               'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Bash ANSI-C and locale quoting.
+    assert_blocked "> \$'/etc/passwd'"                  'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked '> $"/etc/passwd"'                   'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked "echo > \$'/etc/passwd'"             'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    # Device-target redirects (the carve-out is null/zero/full only;
+    # `> /dev/sda` etc. must still block).
+    assert_blocked '> /dev/sda'                         'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'echo zero > /dev/sda1'              'core.filesystem:redirect-truncate-root-home' 'critical'
+}
+
+scenario_mv_bypass_attempts() {
+    # ANSI-C / locale quoting bypass for the mv rule. mv has no
+    # general tier, so without the optional-quote-prefix extension
+    # these slipped through entirely.
+    assert_blocked "mv \$'/etc' /tmp/x"                 'core.filesystem:mv-sensitive-source-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'mv $"/etc" /tmp/x'                  'core.filesystem:mv-sensitive-source-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked "mv \$'/etc/passwd' /tmp/passwd"     'core.filesystem:mv-sensitive-source-root-home' 'critical'
+    assert_blocked 'mv $"/home/user" /tmp/relocated'    'core.filesystem:mv-sensitive-source-root-home' 'critical'
 }
 
 scenario_redirect_bypass_var() {
@@ -937,9 +988,11 @@ run_all() {
         scenario_redirect_root_home \
         scenario_redirect_append_safe \
         scenario_redirect_temp_safe \
+        scenario_redirect_bypass_attempts \
         scenario_redirect_bypass_var \
         scenario_mv_sensitive_root_home \
         scenario_mv_no_false_positive \
+        scenario_mv_bypass_attempts \
         scenario_mv_sensitive_bypass_var \
         scenario_system_disk_default; do
         if declare -F "$scenario" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
